title: What is Python?
content: Python is a programming language used to write code that computers can understand. It is popular for being simple, readable, and powerful.
text: Lesson 1: What is Python? Python is a programming language used to write code that computers can understand. It is popular for being simple, readable, and powerful. Examples: print('Python is fun!')

title: Printing Text
content: The print() function displays text or numbers on the screen. Text must be inside quotes.
text: Lesson 2: Printing Text The print() function displays text or numbers on the screen. Text must be inside quotes. Examples: print('Hello, world!') print('Welcome to Python!')

title: Comments
content: Comments are notes in your code that Python ignores. They start with the # symbol.
examples: ['# This is a comment', "print('Python ignores comments!')"]
text: Lesson 3: Comments Comments are notes in your code that Python ignores. They start with the # symbol. Examples: # This is a comment print('Python ignores comments!')

title: Running Python Code
content: You can run Python code in an editor, a terminal, or an online environment. Code runs line by line, from top to bottom.
text: Lesson 4: Running Python Code You can run Python code in an editor, a terminal, or an online environment. Code runs line by line, from top to bottom. Examples: print('This line runs first!') print('Then this one!')

title: Variables
content: Variables are used to store information. You can give a name to a value using the = sign.
text: Lesson 5: Variables Variables are used to store information. You can give a name to a value using the = sign. Examples: name = 'John' age = 21 print(name, age)

title: Rules for Variable Names
content: Variable names must start with a letter or underscore, cannot have spaces, and are case-sensitive.
text: Lesson 6: Rules for Variable Names Variable names must start with a letter or underscore, cannot have spaces, and are case-sensitive. Examples: my_name = 'John' _count = 5 # my name = 'John' ❌ (invalid)

title: Data Types
content: Common data types in Python are int (numbers), float (decimals), str (text), and bool (True/False).
text: Lesson 7: Data Types Common data types in Python are int (numbers), float (decimals), str (text), and bool (True/False). Examples: age = 18 height = 5.9 name = 'Alex' is_student = True

title: Printing Variables
content: You can print variables by including them in print().
text: Lesson 8: Printing Variables You can print variables by including them in print(). Examples: name = 'John' print('Hello', name)

title: Type Checking
content: Use the type() function to check what data type a variable has.
text: Lesson 9: Type Checking Use the type() function to check what data type a variable has. Examples: x = 10 print(type(x))

title: Numbers
content: Python can handle whole numbers (int) and decimals (float). You can perform arithmetic operations easily.
text: Lesson 10: Numbers Python can handle whole numbers (int) and decimals (float). You can perform arithmetic operations easily. Examples: a = 10 + 5 b = 3.2 * 2

title: Addition and Subtraction
content: Use + for addition and - for subtraction.
text: Lesson 11: Addition and Subtraction Use + for addition and - for subtraction. Examples: x = 5 + 3 y = 10 - 2

title: Multiplication and Division
content: Use * for multiplication and / for division. Division always produces a float.
text: Lesson 12: Multiplication and Division Use * for multiplication and / for division. Division always produces a float. Examples: x = 4 * 2 y = 9 / 3

title: Exponentiation
content: Use ** to raise a number to a power.
text: Lesson 13: Exponentiation Use ** to raise a number to a power. Examples: x = 2 ** 3 # 2 to the power of 3

title: Floor Division and Modulus
content: // divides and rounds down, % gives the remainder.
text: Lesson 14: Floor Division and Modulus // divides and rounds down, % gives the remainder. Examples: print(7 // 2) print(7 % 2)

title: Strings
content: Strings are text in quotes. You can use single or double quotes.
text: Lesson 15: Strings Strings are text in quotes. You can use single or double quotes. Examples: name = 'John' greeting = "Hi there!"

title: Combining Strings
content: You can combine (concatenate) strings using +.
text: Lesson 16: Combining Strings You can combine (concatenate) strings using +. Examples: first = 'Hello' second = 'World' print(first + ' ' + second)

title: String Repetition
content: Use * to repeat a string multiple times.
text: Lesson 17: String Repetition Use * to repeat a string multiple times. Examples: print('Hi!' * 3)

title: String Indexing
content: You can access characters in a string using square brackets []. Indexing starts at 0.
text: Lesson 18: String Indexing You can access characters in a string using square brackets []. Indexing starts at 0. Examples: word = 'Python' print(word[0]) print(word[-1])

title: String Slicing
content: You can get parts of a string using slicing: string[start:end].
text: Lesson 19: String Slicing You can get parts of a string using slicing: string[start:end]. Examples: word = 'Python' print(word[0:3])

title: String Methods
content: Strings have built-in methods to modify text like upper(), lower(), and replace().
text: Lesson 20: String Methods Strings have built-in methods to modify text like upper(), lower(), and replace(). Examples: text = 'Hello' print(text.upper()) print(text.replace('H', 'J'))

title: Getting User Input
content: Use input() to get data from the user. It always returns a string.
text: Lesson 21: Getting User Input Use input() to get data from the user. It always returns a string. Examples: name = input('Enter your name: ') print('Hello', name)

title: Type Conversion
content: Convert data types using int(), float(), or str().
text: Lesson 22: Type Conversion Convert data types using int(), float(), or str(). Examples: age = int(input('Enter your age: ')) print(age + 1)

title: Booleans
content: Booleans represent True or False values.
text: Lesson 23: Booleans Booleans represent True or False values. Examples: is_sunny = True is_raining = False

title: Comparison Operators
content: You can compare values with ==, !=, >, <, >=, and <=.
text: Lesson 24: Comparison Operators You can compare values with ==, !=, >, <, >=, and <=. Examples: print(5 > 3) print(5 == 3)

title: Logical Operators
content: and, or, and not combine or invert boolean values.
text: Lesson 25: Logical Operators and, or, and not combine or invert boolean values. Examples: x = 5 print(x > 0 and x < 10) print(not False)

title: If Statements
content: If statements let you run code only when a condition is true.
text: Lesson 26: If Statements If statements let you run code only when a condition is true. Examples: age = 18 if age >= 18: print('Adult')

title: Else Statements
content: Else runs when the if condition is false.
text: Lesson 27: Else Statements Else runs when the if condition is false. Examples: age = 15 if age >= 18: print('Adult') else: print('Minor')

title: Elif Statements
content: Elif checks additional conditions when the first if is false.
examples: ['grade = 85', 'if grade >= 90:', "    print('A')", 'elif grade >= 80:', "    print('B')", 'else:', "    print('C or lower')"]
text: Lesson 28: Elif Statements Elif checks additional conditions when the first if is false. Examples: grade = 85 if grade >= 90: print('A') elif grade >= 80: print('B') else: print('C or lower')

title: Indentation
content: Python uses indentation (spaces) to mark code blocks instead of braces {}.
text: Lesson 29: Indentation Python uses indentation (spaces) to mark code blocks instead of braces {}. Examples: if True: print('This line is indented')

title: Nested If Statements
content: You can put if statements inside other if statements.
examples: ['x = 10', 'if x > 5:', '    if x < 15:', "        print('x is between 5 and 15')"]
text: Lesson 30: Nested If Statements You can put if statements inside other if statements. Examples: x = 10 if x > 5: if x < 15: print('x is between 5 and 15')

title: While Loops
content: A while loop repeats code as long as a condition is true.
text: Lesson 31: While Loops A while loop repeats code as long as a condition is true. Examples: count = 0 while count < 3: print('Count is', count) count += 1

title: For Loops
content: A for loop is used to loop through a sequence like a list, string, or range of numbers.
text: Lesson 32: For Loops A for loop is used to loop through a sequence like a list, string, or range of numbers. Examples: for i in range(5): print(i)

title: Range Function
content: The range() function creates a sequence of numbers, usually used in loops.
examples: ['for num in range(3):', '    print(num)', 'for num in range(2, 6):', '    print(num)']
text: Lesson 33: Range Function The range() function creates a sequence of numbers, usually used in loops. Examples: for num in range(3): print(num) for num in range(2, 6): print(num)

title: Breaking Out of Loops
content: Use break to stop a loop early when a condition is met.
examples: ['for i in range(5):', '    if i == 3:', '        break', '    print(i)']
text: Lesson 34: Breaking Out of Loops Use break to stop a loop early when a condition is met. Examples: for i in range(5): if i == 3: break print(i)

title: Skipping with Continue
content: Use continue to skip to the next iteration of a loop.
examples: ['for i in range(5):', '    if i == 2:', '        continue', '    print(i)']
text: Lesson 35: Skipping with Continue Use continue to skip to the next iteration of a loop. Examples: for i in range(5): if i == 2: continue print(i)

title: Nested Loops
content: You can put one loop inside another loop. This is useful for working with grids or pairs of items.
examples: ['for i in range(2):', '    for j in range(3):', '        print(i, j)']
text: Lesson 36: Nested Loops You can put one loop inside another loop. This is useful for working with grids or pairs of items. Examples: for i in range(2): for j in range(3): print(i, j)

title: Lists
content: Lists store multiple values in a single variable. Lists are ordered and can be changed.
text: Lesson 37: Lists Lists store multiple values in a single variable. Lists are ordered and can be changed. Examples: fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry'] print(fruits[0]) fruits.append('mango')

title: Accessing List Elements
content: Access list elements using their index. Indexing starts at 0.
text: Lesson 38: Accessing List Elements Access list elements using their index. Indexing starts at 0. Examples: numbers = [10, 20, 30] print(numbers[1])

title: Changing List Elements
content: You can change an item in a list by assigning a new value to its index.
text: Lesson 39: Changing List Elements You can change an item in a list by assigning a new value to its index. Examples: colors = ['red', 'blue', 'green'] colors[1] = 'yellow' print(colors)

title: Looping Through Lists
content: Use a for loop to go through all items in a list.
text: Lesson 40: Looping Through Lists Use a for loop to go through all items in a list. Examples: animals = ['cat', 'dog', 'bird'] for a in animals: print(a)

title: List Methods
content: Lists have useful methods like append(), remove(), and sort().
text: Lesson 41: List Methods Lists have useful methods like append(), remove(), and sort(). Examples: numbers = [3, 1, 4, 2] numbers.sort() print(numbers)

title: List Slicing
content: You can get a part of a list using slicing: list[start:end].
text: Lesson 42: List Slicing You can get a part of a list using slicing: list[start:end]. Examples: numbers = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5] print(numbers[2:5])

title: Tuples
content: Tuples are like lists, but they cannot be changed (immutable). They use parentheses ().
text: Lesson 43: Tuples Tuples are like lists, but they cannot be changed (immutable). They use parentheses (). Examples: colors = ('red', 'blue', 'green') print(colors[0])

title: Dictionaries
content: Dictionaries store key-value pairs. You can use keys to look up values.
text: Lesson 44: Dictionaries Dictionaries store key-value pairs. You can use keys to look up values. Examples: person = {'name': 'John', 'age': 21} print(person['name'])

title: Adding and Changing Dictionary Items
content: You can add or modify dictionary values using their keys.
text: Lesson 45: Adding and Changing Dictionary Items You can add or modify dictionary values using their keys. Examples: person = {'name': 'John', 'age': 21} person['city'] = 'San Diego' person['age'] = 22

title: Looping Through Dictionaries
content: You can loop through keys, values, or both in a dictionary.
examples: ["person = {'name': 'John', 'age': 21}", 'for key, value in person.items():', '    print(key, value)']
text: Lesson 46: Looping Through Dictionaries You can loop through keys, values, or both in a dictionary. Examples: person = {'name': 'John', 'age': 21} for key, value in person.items(): print(key, value)

title: Sets
content: Sets store unique items and are unordered. They are useful when you don’t want duplicates.
text: Lesson 47: Sets Sets store unique items and are unordered. They are useful when you don’t want duplicates. Examples: fruits = {'apple', 'banana', 'apple'} print(fruits)

title: Adding and Removing from Sets
content: Use add() to add items and remove() to remove items from a set.
text: Lesson 48: Adding and Removing from Sets Use add() to add items and remove() to remove items from a set. Examples: colors = {'red', 'green'} colors.add('blue') colors.remove('red')

title: Functions
content: Functions are reusable blocks of code that perform specific tasks. Define them with def.
text: Lesson 49: Functions Functions are reusable blocks of code that perform specific tasks. Define them with def. Examples: def greet(): print('Hello!') greet()

title: Function Parameters
content: Functions can take parameters, which are values you pass in.
text: Lesson 50: Function Parameters Functions can take parameters, which are values you pass in. Examples: def greet(name): print('Hello,', name) greet('John')

title: Return Values
content: Functions can return values using the return keyword.
examples: ['def add(a, b):', '    return a + b', '', 'result = add(3, 4)', 'print(result)']
text: Lesson 51: Return Values Functions can return values using the return keyword. Examples: def add(a, b): return a + b result = add(3, 4) print(result)

title: Default Parameters
content: You can give function parameters default values.
text: Lesson 52: Default Parameters You can give function parameters default values. Examples: def greet(name='friend'): print('Hello,', name) greet() greet('John')

title: Scope of Variables
content: Variables created inside functions exist only inside them (local scope).
examples: ['def example():', '    x = 5', '    print(x)', '', 'example()', '# print(x) ❌ This would cause an error']
text: Lesson 53: Scope of Variables Variables created inside functions exist only inside them (local scope). Examples: def example(): x = 5 print(x) example() # print(x) ❌ This would cause an error

title: Importing Modules
content: Modules are files that contain Python code you can reuse. Use import to include them.
text: Lesson 54: Importing Modules Modules are files that contain Python code you can reuse. Use import to include them. Examples: import math print(math.sqrt(16))

title: Random Numbers
content: The random module can generate random numbers or choices.
text: Lesson 55: Random Numbers The random module can generate random numbers or choices. Examples: import random print(random.randint(1, 10)) print(random.choice(['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']))

title: Working with Strings and Numbers Together
content: You can’t add strings and numbers directly, but you can use commas in print() or convert using str().
examples: ['age = 21', "print('I am', age, 'years old.')", "print('I am ' + str(age) + ' years old.')"]
text: Lesson 56: Working with Strings and Numbers Together You can’t add strings and numbers directly, but you can use commas in print() or convert using str(). Examples: age = 21 print('I am', age, 'years old.') print('I am ' + str(age) + ' years old.')

title: File Reading
content: Use open() and read() to read text from a file.
text: Lesson 57: File Reading Use open() and read() to read text from a file. Examples: file = open('example.txt', 'r') content = file.read() print(content) file.close()

title: File Writing
content: You can write to files using the 'w' mode with open(). Be careful—it replaces the file’s contents.
text: Lesson 58: File Writing You can write to files using the 'w' mode with open(). Be careful—it replaces the file’s contents. Examples: file = open('example.txt', 'w') file.write('Hello file!') file.close()

title: With Statement for Files
content: Using with automatically closes the file when done.
text: Lesson 59: With Statement for Files Using with automatically closes the file when done. Examples: with open('example.txt', 'r') as file: print(file.read())

title: Putting It All Together
content: You can combine variables, loops, conditionals, and functions to make small programs.
examples: ['def greet_users(users):', '    for name in users:', '        if len(name) > 3:', "            print('Hello,', name)", '', "greet_users(['Ali', 'John', 'Bo'])"]
text: Lesson 60: Putting It All Together You can combine variables, loops, conditionals, and functions to make small programs. Examples: def greet_users(users): for name in users: if len(name) > 3: print('Hello,', name) greet_users(['Ali', 'John', 'Bo'])
